segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a detailed anatomical model of a human heart, mounted on a stand, illuminated against a dark background, emphasizing its intricate structure with visible arteries and chambers. The scene transitions to a dimly lit, abstract shot of a mechanical device with tubes and connectors, suggesting a medical or scientific apparatus. This is followed by a series of black-and-white archival footage clips showing a surgical team in an operating room, with a sign reading 'OPERATING ROOM 1', and close-ups of medical procedures involving a patient under drapes and a glass medical device being handled. The footage then shifts to color, showing a complex mechanical device with rotating parts and tubes, possibly a heart-lung machine, followed by a modern laboratory setting where a man in a white lab coat examines a small mechanical component under a magnifying lamp. The scene changes to a man in casual attire holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, then to a robotic arm with a similar device attached, suggesting advanced medical technology. The video concludes with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' surrounded by animated pills, with the text 'Presented By Lilly' and 'A MEDICINE COMPANY' below, set against a dark background with floating pills and capsules.

segments.caption: The video opens with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' presented by Lilly, surrounded by animated pills and capsules, setting a medical and scientific tone. The scene transitions to archival footage of a surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure under bright lights, with a focus on the intricate work being done. This is followed by a modern-day shot of a person in scrubs carrying a white 'Playmate Plus' cooler, suggesting the transport of sensitive medical equipment. The narrative then shifts to a black screen with animated graphics illustrating the global shortage of heart transplants: 6,000 performed annually versus 1,000,000 requested worldwide and 600,000 in the U.S., emphasizing the critical need for alternatives. The video then introduces William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon at Texas Heart Institute, seated in a museum-like setting with anatomical models in the background, speaking directly to the camera. He is later seen walking through a hallway, passing a glass display case containing medical artifacts, before returning to his seat. Archival footage reappears, showing a surgeon in a lab coat working on a patient in an operating room, followed by a close-up of a mechanical device with tubes and connectors, likely part of a heart-lung machine. The segment concludes with a vintage animation of a human body with a highlighted circulatory system, transitioning to a full-body silhouette with glowing points, symbolizing the integration of artificial systems into the human form.

segments.caption: The video opens with a vintage animation of a human body, highlighting the circulatory system with glowing points, transitioning into a close-up of a detailed anatomical heart model with visible chambers and arteries. The scene shifts to a modern museum setting where William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon, is seated and speaking, gesturing toward a display case containing medical artifacts. The camera then focuses on a museum display case showcasing a mechanical heart device with tubes and connectors, labeled 'THORATEC,' alongside informational plaques and diagrams. Archival black-and-white footage follows, showing a man in a lab coat, identified as Dr. Cooley, meticulously working on a mechanical heart component under a magnifying lamp. The video then cuts to a vintage color clip of a surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure on a patient with a mechanical heart device connected to their chest. The patient is shown lying on the operating table, intubated and monitored, with a close-up of a heart-lung machine and a graph recording vital signs. The sequence ends with a close-up of the patient’s face, showing signs of distress, as medical staff monitor the procedure.

segments.caption: The video opens with archival footage from April 1969, showing a man in a white shirt and tie speaking directly to the camera, with the text 'Network News Film April 1969' overlaid. The scene transitions to close-ups of dark rectangular objects, possibly medical components, followed by a surgical view of a heart with tubes and clamps, indicating an open-heart procedure. A medical monitor with a circular screen displaying a faint signal is shown, suggesting real-time monitoring. The video then cuts to a modern setting where William Cohn, MD, is seated in a museum-like environment, gesturing as he speaks, with a glass display case containing an anatomical heart model beside him. The footage returns to surgical close-ups, showing a mechanical valve being inserted into a heart, followed by a detailed view of a white, spherical artificial heart component. The segment concludes with William Cohn continuing his interview, using hand gestures to emphasize his points, while the background reveals a well-lit, educational space with medical exhibits.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a white mechanical heart device with transparent chambers and tubing, labeled 'SynCardia', resting on a red surface next to a gray 'Freedom' device. A hand gestures toward it, emphasizing its design. The scene transitions to a woman in a red jacket and black pants, smiling as she walks through a hospital corridor, carrying a backpack and a cane, suggesting mobility and independence. Next, a detailed anatomical heart model with red and blue vessels is shown, followed by a mechanical heart device with a perforated casing being tested in a lab setting, with 'European Patent Office' text in the corner. A man in a blue blazer, identified as William Cohn, MD, speaks while gesturing in a museum-like space with medical displays. The video then shows a close-up of a hand holding a white, spherical artificial heart component, followed by a patient in a hospital gown in an ICU room, connected to monitors, with medical staff attending to him. The scene shifts to a surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure on a patient under bright lights. A hand is shown connecting a small black device with a cable to a belt, labeled 'CARMAT', indicating a portable power or control unit. The same man in the blue blazer reappears, speaking in the same museum setting, with a display case of anatomical models behind him. The final shot shows a man in a black shirt attaching a black pouch to his belt, with a medical device connected via a cable, suggesting a wearable system for artificial heart support.

segments.caption: The video opens with William Cohn, MD, seated in a museum-like environment, speaking thoughtfully while gesturing with his hand near his face. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip viewed through a circular lens, showing a dimly lit mechanical apparatus with tubes and connectors, possibly part of an early medical machine. The video returns to William Cohn, now with a close-up of his face as he continues speaking, with a colorful anatomical heart model visible in the background. The setting shifts to Brisbane, Australia, where a man walks toward a building labeled 'Pathology Queensland' and 'Clinical Sciences,' carrying a black case. Inside, he enters a modern laboratory where a complex mechanical device with tubes and a rotating component is being operated. The camera focuses on the device, revealing its intricate design and connections. William Cohn walks through the lab, observing the equipment. The scene cuts to Daniel Timms, Chief Executive Officer & Chief Financial Officer of BiVACOR, seated in the lab, speaking directly to the camera. He then holds a small, metallic, spherical artificial heart component, examining it closely. The video concludes with a 3D animation showing the artificial heart component being integrated into a human heart, highlighting its placement and function within the circulatory system.

segments.caption: The video begins with a detailed 3D animation of a mechanical heart device, labeled 'Bivacor', showcasing its internal components as they disassemble and reassemble in a clean, white environment. The animation highlights the device’s dual-chamber structure with blue and red fluid flows representing blood circulation, and a central rotating impeller mechanism. The camera zooms into the core, revealing copper coils, green insulating layers, and a gold-colored rotor, emphasizing the device’s advanced engineering. The scene transitions to a man in a blue shirt, identified as Daniel Timms, seated in a modern office or lab setting with graphs and equipment in the background, speaking directly to the camera. The animation returns, showing a top-down view of the device’s internal flow dynamics, with red and blue particles simulating blood movement. The video then cuts to real-world footage: a close-up of a white catheter-like tube being manipulated, followed by a gloved hand holding a small, metallic, turbine-like component, possibly part of the device. An animated diagram illustrates the device’s integration into a human heart, with tubes connecting to the atria and ventricles. The final shot shows Daniel Timms holding the actual Bivacor device in one hand and its gold-colored rotor in the other, demonstrating the physical components in a well-lit, professional environment. The on-screen text 'Bivacor' remains visible throughout, reinforcing the brand identity.

active_entities.Daniel Timms: A man in a blue shirt, seated in a modern office or lab setting, speaking to the camera and later holding the Bivacor device and its rotor, identified as Chief Executive Officer & Chief Financial Officer of BiVACOR.

discovered_new_entities.Bivacor Rotor (Physical): A gold-colored, multi-bladed rotor component of the Bivacor device, held separately by Daniel Timms, demonstrating its intricate mechanical structure.
discovered_new_entities.Catheter-Like Tube: A white, flexible tube with a metallic tip, shown in close-up, likely used for implantation or connection to the Bivacor device, manipulated in a sterile environment.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of Daniel Timms, a man in a blue shirt, holding a small, gold-colored mechanical component—the Bivacor Rotor (Physical)—in his hands. He rotates it carefully, showcasing its intricate, multi-bladed design and concentric grooves, while his other hand holds the Bivacor Device (Physical), a compact, dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing and metallic housing. The camera zooms in on the rotor, emphasizing its precision engineering. He then aligns the rotor with the device, demonstrating how it fits into the central chamber. The scene transitions to a laboratory setting, revealing a complex fluid dynamics setup with transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected to a network of valves and pumps. These tubes, labeled with handwritten text such as 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', simulate a circulatory system, with fluid levels rising and falling rhythmically. The camera pans across the setup, showing the integration of the Bivacor device into the system, with the rotor spinning inside as fluid flows through the tubes. The environment is a modern, well-lit lab with stainless steel surfaces, medical equipment, and a black case on the table, suggesting a testing or development phase. The overall atmosphere is technical and focused, highlighting the functionality and engineering of the Bivacor device in a controlled, experimental environment.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a complex fluid dynamics testing apparatus, featuring transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected by valves and pumps, labeled with handwritten text such as 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', simulating a circulatory system. The camera pans across the setup, revealing a white robotic arm with articulated joints, mounted on a metal frame, interacting with the system. A man in a blue shirt and jeans, identified as Daniel Timms, is seen adjusting components on the lab bench, which includes a black case and a small anatomical heart model. He carefully handles a gold-colored, multi-bladed rotor component, the Bivacor Rotor (Physical), and inserts it into the Bivacor Device (Physical), a dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing. The robotic arm then manipulates a catheter-like tube, demonstrating the integration of the device into the fluid system. The lab environment is modern and well-lit, with stainless steel tables, various tools, and a sign reading 'DISCOVERIES PRODUCED HERE FOR THE COMMON GOOD' visible in the background. The man observes the robotic arm’s precise movements, occasionally adjusting settings or components, showcasing the testing and functionality of the Bivacor device in a simulated circulatory environment.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a tablet displaying real-time data from a medical device, including motor position, current, speed, and power, with a hand adjusting settings. The screen shows waveforms and controls for pulse amplitude and rate, indicating active monitoring. The scene transitions to a robotic arm interacting with a fluid dynamics testing apparatus, simulating blood flow through transparent tubes, with a hand adjusting a valve. A 3D animation then illustrates the Bivacor artificial heart inside a human torso, highlighting its patented rotor position control and internal fluid dynamics with red and blue blood flow. The video cuts to a car driving through a suburban area, showing a modern building and trees, before shifting to a man driving, focused on the road. The scene changes to a workshop where the same man, now in a blue shirt and jeans, assembles a mechanical device with tubes and connectors, using tools and components laid out on a wooden workbench. The video includes a nostalgic photo of two men holding a long white object, possibly a prototype, followed by a top-down view of the disassembled device parts on the workbench, including a rotor, valves, and tubing, suggesting a hands-on engineering process.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands assembling a mechanical component on a wooden workbench, part of a larger device. The scene transitions to a man in a blue shirt, identified as Daniel Timms, working in a well-lit workshop environment with a pegboard wall holding various tools. He carefully disassembles and inspects a beige, multi-port mechanical device—identified as the Bivacor Device (Physical)—removing a central rotor component, the Bivacor Rotor (Physical), which is gold-colored with concentric grooves. He examines the rotor closely, turning it in his hands, then holds up a circular disc with a central brass pin, likely a part of the rotor assembly. The setting shifts to a different location where Daniel Timms is seated on a black leather couch, facing the camera, speaking directly to the viewer in what appears to be an interview or explanatory segment. The background shows a glass partition with reflections of lab equipment, suggesting a modern research or medical facility. His expression is engaged and informative, indicating he is explaining the function or development of the device. The lighting is bright and even, emphasizing his face and gestures as he communicates.

segments.caption: The video opens with Daniel Timms in an interview setting, seated on a black leather couch in a modern indoor space with a glass partition reflecting lab equipment behind him. The scene transitions to a close-up of the Bivacor Device (Physical) integrated into the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, with transparent tubes and valves simulating a circulatory system. Two men, one in a gray sweater and another in a blue shirt, are shown collaborating at a workstation with dual monitors displaying complex software interfaces, likely controlling or analyzing the device. The footage then cuts to a Bloomberg news article highlighting the European approval of the first total artificial heart, with a photo of a Carmat employee holding a beige, perforated artificial heart. This is followed by a dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office,' showing a CARMAT Device being tested on a mechanical platform with a gold-colored, perforated component. A tablet interface displays real-time data, including pressure waveforms and patient metrics, with French labels such as 'Pression relative ventriculaire gauche (mmHg)'. The scene shifts back to the lab, where the Bivacor Device is shown in full context with the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, and a man in a blue shirt is seen working in the background. A 3D animation of the Bivacor device is shown inside a human torso, demonstrating its integration with the circulatory system. The video then features a SynCardia device on a red surface, followed by a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves training with a punching bag, wearing a portable medical pouch connected to a device. The final shot shows Daniel Timms in a dark blazer, holding the Bivacor Device (Physical) and a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating the device’s connection to the human heart, with the robotic arm and testing apparatus visible in the background.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in a modern laboratory setting, where a robotic arm with articulated joints (Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart) is shown interacting with a fluid dynamics testing apparatus, manipulating a catheter-like tube within transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid. A man in a dark blazer (Daniel Timms) holds a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating integration with the Bivacor Device (Physical), which is connected to the apparatus via white tubing. The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including graphs for 'Pressure Sensors', 'Flow Sensors', 'Pump Controls & Data', and 'Pressure-Volume Loops', with a man in a gray sweater (Man in Gray Sweater) using a mouse to control or analyze the data alongside a man in a blue shirt (Man in Blue Shirt (Workstation)) observing. The setting then shifts to a museum-like environment, where William Cohn, MD, seated in a chair, speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands, with a glass display case (Museum Display Case) containing anatomical models behind him. The video concludes with end credits on a black background, featuring the 'Lilly' logo, animated pills and capsules, and text listing production roles, followed by a teal 'O' logo on a dark blue background.
